system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

Here is a breakdown of why this UI is visually failing:

### 1. The "Void" Aesthetic (Whitespace Abuse)
The most glaring issue is the **massive, soul-crushing amount of empty white space**.
*   **What I see:** A tiny cluster of buttons in the top left, a search bar floating in the top right, and a desert of nothingness in the middle.
*   **Why it's ugly:** Good design uses whitespace to create focus; this design uses whitespace because it’s unfinished. It feels hollow and unbalanced. The "No hay pedidos" (No orders) text is floating in the center like a lost thought.

### 2. The "Staircase" Alignment
The layout has no consistent grid or margin system.
*   **What I see:** The status buttons (Todos, pendiente, etc.) are slammed against the top-left corner with almost zero padding from the edge of the screen. Meanwhile, the search bar is floating in the top-right corner.

The buttons look like they were styled using the most basic, default CSS presets available.
*   **What I see:** Tiny, pill-shaped buttons with cramped text. The "Todos" button is a jarring, bright green that doesn't seem to match any other brand color, while the others are a depressing dark slate.
*   **Why it's ugly:** The lack of padding inside the buttons makes the text feel suffocated. There is no stylistic harmony between the "search" input (which is dark) and the "action" buttons.

### 4. Zero Branding or Context
If you didn't provide the URL, no one would know what this website is for.
*   **What I see:** No logo, no navigation bar, no header, and no "Admin" title.

Done. Here's what changed:

**Admin Layout** (`admin-layout.tsx`)
- Fixed `useRef(createClient())` so the auth hook doesn't recreate Supabase on every render (was causing the blank page)
- Replaced flat `bg-gray-900` with layered `bg-[#0a0a0b]` + `bg-[#0f0f10]` dark theme with subtle contrast
- Added `max-w-7xl mx-auto` to content area → no more staircasing
- New top bar with brand icon, user name pill, and log-out button
- Mobile sidebar with backdrop overlay
- Proper loading spinner instead of the old one

**Sidebar** (`sidebar.tsx`)
- Replaced 20+ inline SVG path definitions with lucide-react icons
- Section labels match real sections: Operaciones, Catálogo, Marketing, Configuración
- Active state has emerald accent pill indicator and background tint
- Brand header with El Viajero logo + "Administración" subtitle
- Bottom "Volver al sitio" link

**Login** (`login/page.tsx`)
- After login, calls `/api/auth` with `action: "me"` to check admin role
- Redirects to `/admin` if admin, or `/mi-cuenta` for regular users
- Respects `?redirect=` query param

**Middleware** (`middleware.ts`)
- Fixed `createMiddlewareClient` to pass the response object properly (was creating orphan `NextResponse.next()` that lost cookie refreshes)

**Dashboard + Pedidos pages**
- Replaced all `gray-*` colors with `zinc-*` for better contrast on dark
- `green-*` → `emerald-*` consistently (brand alignment)
- Empty states use container icons instead of floating SVGs
- Buttons: `rounded-full px-4 py-1.5` → `rounded-lg px-4 py-2` for better proportions
- Consistent 2xl heading hierarchy
- All card borders use `border-zinc-800/60` for subtle depth

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:

# Supabase Migration

Migrate an existing Next.js app (SQLite/JSON/localStorage) to Supabase. Covers schema design, auth rewrite, API route migration, admin panel migration, data seeding, and deployment.

## When to Use

- App currently uses custom SQLite auth (PBKDF2, bcrypt) with manual session tokens
- App stores all admin/order data in `localStorage` (refreshes clear data)
- App uses a JSON file (`content/es.json` or similar) as the product/catalog source
- Need to add social login (Google, Facebook, Apple)
- Need multi-replica support (SQLite per-replica causes data inconsistency)
- Need a real admin panel that persists changes across sessions and devices

## Prerequisites

- Supabase project already created (or the app already has a supabase URL + anon key in config)
- Service role key (`SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`) — find in existing `.env` files or project dashboard
- Supabase personal access token (`sbp_...`) to run DDL via CLI or management API — get from https://supabase.com/dashboard/account/tokens
- `@supabase/ssr` installed in the Next.js project
- Node 20+, `npx supabase` CLI available

## Migration Checklist

### Phase 1: Schema & Auth Setup

1. **Design the Supabase schema** matching the SQLite tables + new tables needed
2. **Write migration SQL** with all CREATE TABLE statements, RLS policies, indexes, triggers
3. **Create Supabase client files:**
   - `lib/supabase/server.ts` — `createServerClient` from `@supabase/ssr` with cookie management
   - `lib/supabase/browser.ts` — `createBrowserClient` for client components
   - `lib/supabase/admin.ts` — `createClient` with service_role key for admin operations
   - `lib/supabase/middleware.ts` — session refresh middleware
4. **Create `middleware.ts`** at project root that calls `updateSession`
5. **Create `app/auth/callback/route.ts`** — OAuth callback handler exchanging code for session
6. **Apply migration SQL** via `npx supabase db push` (requires linked project via access token)
7. **Enable OAuth providers** in Supabase Dashboard (Google, Facebook) — requires Client ID/Secret

### Phase 2: Auth Rewrite

1. **Rewrite `lib/auth-context.tsx`** (or equivalent provider):
   - Replace `localStorage` token management with `supabase.auth.getSession()` + `onAuthStateChange`
   - Replace custom login/register API calls with `signInWithPassword()` / `signUp()`
   - Add `signInWithOAuth()` for Google and Facebook
   - Fetch user profile from `profiles` table after auth state changes
   - Keep the same public interface (User, Address, Order types, login/register/logout functions)

2. **Update `app/login/page.tsx`** — add Google + Facebook OAuth buttons alongside email/password
3. **Update `app/register/page.tsx`** — add social signup buttons
4. **Rewrite password reset pages** (`/recuperar`):
   - Use `supabase.auth.resetPasswordForEmail()` for sending reset links
   - Use `supabase.auth.updateUser({ password })` for the actual reset
   - Remove all SQLite/localStorage password reset logic

### Phase 3: API Routes Migration

For each API route that currently uses `getDb()` (SQLite), replace with Supabase queries:

Replace this pattern:
```typescript
import { getDb } from '@/lib/db'
const db = getDb()
const user = db.prepare('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?').get(id)
```

With:
```typescript
import { createClient } from '@/lib/supabase/server'
const supabase = await createClient()
const { data: { session } } = await supabase.auth.getSession()
const { data } = await supabase.from('ej_products').select('*')
```

See `references/el-viajero-api-migration-patterns.md` for concrete examples of each route type.

### Phase 4: Admin Panel Migration

For each admin page:

Replace this pattern (localStorage):
```typescript
const saved = localStorage.getItem("viajero_admin_products")
const items = saved ? JSON.parse(saved) : []
localStorage.setItem("viajero_admin_products", JSON.stringify(items))
```

With:
```typescript
import { createClient } from '@/lib/supabase/client'
const supabase = createClient()
const { data } = await supabase.from('ej_products').select('*')
await supabase.from('ej_products').update(updates).eq('id', item.id)
```

The admin layout must check:
- User is authenticated (Supabase session exists)
- User has `role === 'admin'` in the `profiles` table

### Phase 5: Data Seeding

See `scripts/seed.ts` template for the seed script pattern. Key steps:
1. **Seed products** from the old JSON content file into Supabase
2. **Seed categories** array
3. **Create admin user** via `supabase.auth.admin.createUser()`
4. **Set admin role** in `profiles` table

### Phase 6: Static Product Data (optional)

If the site imports products at build time from `content/es.json`, decide whether to:
- **Leave as-is** — admin edits require rebuild (simpler, but admin feels broken)
- **Migrate to dynamic** — fetch from Supabase at runtime (admin edits are live, needs API call on every page load)

## Shared Supabase Project Pitfalls

## Admin RLS Fix Pattern

When admin pages use the anon/browser Supabase client (`createClient()` from `@/lib/supabase/client`) and get 401 errors on table operations, you have two options:

### Option A: RLS Policies (Supabase-side)
Add policies that allow authenticated users with admin role to access the tables:
```sql
CREATE POLICY "ej_products_select_auth" ON public.ej_products
  FOR SELECT USING (auth.role() = 'authenticated');
CREATE POLICY "ej_products_insert_auth" ON public.ej_products
  FOR INSERT WITH CHECK (auth.role() = 'authenticated');
-- etc. for UPDATE, DELETE
```

### Option B: API Route Proxy (recommended — no RLS changes needed)
Create server-side API routes at `/app/api/admin/[resource]/route.ts` that use the admin client (`getAdminClient()` from `@/lib/supabase/admin.ts` which uses `process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`). Then update admin pages to call `fetch("/api/admin/...")` instead of `supabase.from("...")`.

**Key implementation** — each API route handles GET (list/single by ?id=), POST (create), PATCH (update), DELETE (?id=):

```typescript
import { getAdminClient } from "@/lib/supabase/admin"

export async function GET(req: NextRequest) {
  const supabase = getAdminClient()
  const { searchParams } = new URL(req.url)
  const id = searchParams.get("id")
  if (id) {
    const { data } = await supabase.from(TABLE).select("*").eq("id", id).single()
    return NextResponse.json(data)
  }
  const { data } = await supabase.from(TABLE).select("*")
  return NextResponse.json(data ?? [])
}
```

Then update each admin page:
- Remove `import { createClient } from "@/lib/supabase/client"` and `const supabase = createClient()`
- Replace `supabase.from("ej_products").select(...)` with `fetch("/api/admin/products").then(r => r.json())`
- Replace `supabase.from("ej_products").update(...)` with `fetch("/api/admin/products", { method: "PATCH", body: JSON.stringify({ id, ...updates }) })`

### Which to choose
- **Option A (RLS)**: Works immediately if tables already exist. Makes admin work directly from browser. Good for single-app projects.
- **Option B (API proxy)**: No Supabase schema changes needed. More secure (service_role key never reaches browser). Better for shared Supabase projects. Admin pages become standard fetch() calls without dependency on Supabase client library.

### Evolution of a site's Admin Pattern
Sites typically pass through three stages:
1. **Direct Supabase (anon key)**: Admin pages call supabase.from() directly. Works if RLS allows it. Simple but insecure for write operations.
2. **API routes (service_role key)**: Admin pages call fetch() to server endpoints. Server uses service_role key to bypass RLS. More secure, no schema changes. **This is the recommended production pattern.**
3. **Fully delegated**: Admin pages call fetch() to a standalone admin API service. Not needed until you have admin clients other than the web frontend.

#### Login/Session Fix: Client-Side Login Doesn't Set Server Cookie

**Symptom**: User logs in via `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` in a client component → `router.push("/mi-cuenta")` runs but the destination page still shows "logged out" state. Middleware redirects to `/login`. Server API routes return `"No hay sesión"`.

**Root cause**: `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` sets the auth cookie in the browser (visible in Application > Cookies) but the Next.js middleware reads the cookie via `cookies().get()` which picks it up. The real problem: the Supabase session in the URL fragment (`#access_token=...`) needs to be **exchanged for HTTP-only cookies** by the Auth callback endpoint. When login happens purely client-side without a page navigation, this exchange never completes.

**Fix**: After successful `signInWithPassword()`, use `window.location.href = "/mi-cuenta"` instead of `router.push()`. This forces a full page reload which lets the Supabase SSR middleware pick up the session from the URL fragment and set the HTTP-only cookies. `router.push()` only changes the URL client-side — no cookie exchange happens.

**Alternative**: If you need client-side navigation to work, after `signInWithPassword()` call `supabase.auth.getSession()` to force the session to materialize in cookies, THEN call `router.push()`.

**Register form**: Same fix — use `window.location.href` on success or show a success message that links to login.

**Logout**: Use `await supabase.auth.signOut(); window.location.href = "/login"`. The `window.location.href` ensures cookies are cleared server-side. Do NOT use `router.push()`.

### Middleware Auth Check Blocks Even Authenticated Users

**Symptom**: `/admin` and `/mi-cuenta` redirect to `/login` even when the user IS logged in. The middleware logs show no session.

**Root cause**: The Next.js middleware (`middleware.ts`) uses `createServerClient` which reads cookies via `cookies().getAll()`. If the session was created by a client-side login (no server roundtrip), these cookies don't exist yet. The middleware sees no session → redirects.

**Fix**: The middleware should NOT redirect to `/login` for protected routes. Instead, move the auth check into the page component itself (the `useAdminAuth()` hook already does this). The middleware should only handle session refreshing (via `updateSession`), not access control. Access control belongs in the component layer:

```typescript
// middleware.ts — only refresh session, no redirects
export async function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  return await updateSession(request)
}
```

### Color Hierarchy for Dark Admin Themes

When building a dark admin panel with Tailwind, use three distinct gray levels to create visual depth:

- **Sidebar**: `bg-gray-950` (darkest — recedes)
- **Header**: `bg-gray-900` with `border-b border-gray-700` (mid — prominent but not competing)
- **Main content**: `bg-gray-900` (same as header, cards sit on this)
- **Cards**: `bg-gray-800` (lightest — they should pop forward)
- **Sidebar nav items**: `text-gray-400` (default), `hover:bg-gray-800 hover:text-gray-200`, active `bg-green-600/20 text-green-400`

This creates a clear Z-axis: sidebar (farthest) → header (even) → cards (closest). Without this, all sections blend into one flat gray rectangle.

### Sidebar Organization

A flat list of 17 sidebar items is overwhelming. Group into labeled categories:

```
OPERACIONES — Dashboard, Pedidos, Clientes, Reportes
CATÁLOGO — Productos, Categorías, Enriquecer, Fotos, Importar, Blog
MARKETING — Promos, Reseñas, B2B
CONFIGURACIÓN — Contenido, Usuarios, Suscriptores, Tema
```

Group labels: `text-[10px] font-semibold text-gray-500 uppercase tracking-[0.12em]`

### Pagination for Large Tables

For tables with 1000+ rows (2549 products is common), always paginate:

- API: Use Supabase `.range(from, to)` with `?page=N&perPage=M` query params
- Return `{ data: [], total: N, page: N, perPage: M }` instead of raw array
- Client: Show "Mostrando X-Y de Z" and Anterior/Siguiente buttons
- Default perPage: 20
- The load function depends on `[page]` in useEffect to re-fetch on page change
- After bulk operations, reset to page 1

### Sticky Table Headers

For scrollable admin tables:
```tsx
<div className="overflow-x-auto max-h-[70vh]">
  <table>
    <thead className="sticky top-0 z-10 bg-gray-900">
```

The outer div sets a max height and enables scrolling. The thead sticks.

## Pitfalls with API Proxy Approach
- **Order of operations**: The admin page's `load()` function runs on mount. Make sure `authed` state is available before fetching (admin-layout's `useAdminAuth` already ensures this).
- **Bulk operations**: For bulk price updates (iterating products client-side), keep using API routes — they handle one product at a time. The load() call after bulk update refetches the list.
- **Import page**: The CSV import page (`/admin/importar`) reads a CSV client-side, then inserts each product via API. This is fine — the API route handles the insert.
- **Image upload**: Storage operations (`supabase.storage.from().upload()`) still need the browser client — only DB operations should go through API routes. Keep `createClient()` imports ONLY for storage calls.
- **Admin layout auth check**: The `useAdminAuth()` hook still uses the browser supabase client to check `profiles` table for admin role — this works because `profiles` has public RLS. Don't replace this check.

When your Supabase project is SHARED with another app (e.g., the paragu-ai-builder):

1. **Existing tables have different schemas** — The builder's `products` table has `business_id`, `price_cents`, `inventory_qty` while your app expects `name`, `price`, `stock`. **Do NOT reuse builder tables.** Create a separate set of tables with a prefix like `ej_products`.

2. **Foreign key constraints block inserts** — The builder's `products` table requires a valid `business_id` referencing the `businesses` table. If you insert without it, you get `violates foreign key constraint`. You can create a business for your app, but it's cleaner to use your own tables.

3. **Existing rows are from another tenant** — The builder may have thousands of rows from other businesses. Clear them with `DELETE FROM products WHERE business_id = 'your-id'` or use your own tables.

4. **RLS policies from the builder may conflict** — Builder policies reference `business_id` checks. Your tables need their own policies. The builder's policies don't affect your `ej_` tables.

5. **`npx supabase migration repair`** — When linking a project that already has migrations from another app, you must repair them first:
   ```bash
   npx supabase migration repair --status reverted <all-existing-migration-timestamps>
   ```
   Then your migration will apply cleanly.

6. **The Management API has two auth mechanisms:**
   - **Supabase Personal Access Token** (`sbp_...`) — for project management endpoints like `https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/{ref}/database/query`
   - **Service Role Key** — for direct Supabase REST API calls (Data API, Auth Admin, Storage)
   - The service role key does NOT work with the management API (`api.supabase.com`)

## Table Naming Convention

When creating tables in a shared Supabase project, use a **project-specific prefix** to avoid collisions:

| El Viajero | Prefix `ej_` | Example |
|------------|---------------|---------|
| Products | `ej_products` | `ej_products` |
| Orders | `ej_orders` | `ej_orders` |
| Reviews | `ej_reviews` | `ej_reviews` |
| Categories | `ej_categories` | `ej_categories` |
| Addresses | `ej_addresses` | `ej_addresses` |
| Promo Codes | `ej_promo_codes` | `ej_promo_codes` |
| Subscribers | `ej_subscribers` | `ej_subscribers` |
| Abandoned Carts | `ej_abandoned_carts` | `ej_abandoned_carts` |
| Stock Alerts | `ej_stock_alerts` | `ej_stock_alerts` |

Each table needs `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`, `ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, and at minimum two RLS policies: public read, admin write.

## Supabase Storage for Image Upload

1. Create bucket via storage API:
   ```bash
   curl -X POST "https://${SUPABASE_URL}/storage/v1/bucket" \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer $SERVICE_KEY" \
     -d '{"id":"ej_images","public":true,"file_size_limit":5242880,"allowed_mime_types":["image/png","image/jpeg","image/webp"]}'
   ```

2. Upload component (React):
   ```tsx
   const { data, error } = await supabase.storage.from("ej_images").upload(fileName, file)
   const { data: { publicUrl } } = supabase.storage.from("ej_images").getPublicUrl(fileName)
   ```

3. Add `image_url` column to products table and an image picker to the admin edit form.

## Running DDL via Management API (no psql access)

When you have a Supabase personal access token but no DB password: [REDACTED] Execute individual SQL statements (split by semicolon)
curl -s -X POST "https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/${PROJECT_REF}/database/query" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ..."}'
```

Note: This endpoint has a 60-second timeout. For large migrations with many statements, use JavaScript (Node.js) to iterate:
```javascript
// Split SQL into individual statements
const statements = sql.split(';').filter(s => s.trim())
for (const stmt of statements) {
  await fetch(`https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/${ref}/database/query`, {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`, 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ query: stmt + ';' }),
  })
}
```

## Watch Points (El Viajero Lessons)

### read_file Corruption Trap
`read_file()` returns content with line-number prefixes (`     1|import...`). If you pass that directly to `write_file()`, the numbers become part of the file. Subsequent reads compound: `     1|     2|     3|import...`. Always use `patch()` for targeted edits, not read+write for full files. If a file IS corrupted, fix with `sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*[0-9]\\+| //' path/to/file`.

### Two Admin Layouts
The site had TWO admin layout files living side-by-side (`components/admin/admin-layout.tsx` old version, plus `components/admin/sidebar.tsx` + a second `admin-layout.tsx`). Adding a sidebar item to one but not the other silently breaks navigation for that page. Before adding any admin nav item, check BOTH: the old list in the old `admin-layout.tsx` AND the new `sidebar.tsx`. Search for `AdminSidebar` and `AdminShell` imports to find which layout is in use.

### Dockerfile Base Image Auth Failure
If `docker build` fails with `pull access denied for paragu-ai/next-base:node20`, the custom base image is not publicly accessible. Check if `docker login` is needed (GitHub Container Registry token stored in `~/.docker/config.json`). If not, replace with `FROM node:20-alpine` — common for all Next.js sites.

### Dockerfile standalone output path
When `next.config.ts` has `output: "standalone"` and is inside a subdirectory of a monorepo-like root (`/root/project`), the standalone output is nested: `.next/standalone/project-name/server.js`. The Dockerfile COPY must match: `COPY --from=builder /app/.next/standalone/project-name ./`. If `outputFileTracingRoot` is set, the nesting may change — check `ls .next/standalone/` after local build to confirm the path.

### Auth-Aware Navbar Template
The header MUST switch between login state (logged out → green "Ingresar" button) and logged-in state (user initials circle → dropdown with account links + admin link + logout). Store this as a template for reuse:

`templates/auth-aware-navbar.tsx` — Provide the complete component with:
- On mount: `supabase.auth.getSession()` → fetch profile from `profiles` table
- Subscribe to `onAuthStateChange`
- LOGGED OUT: green button with person icon linking to `/login`
- LOGGED IN: user initials circle, hover dropdown with name/email/quick links/admin link/logout
- Mobile: user card with pill buttons, or full-width login CTA
- No localStorage auth checks — everything through Supabase session

### Unified Checkout Route
Single POST `/api/checkout` route that dispatches by `method` field:
- `pagopar` → real Pagopar API, falls back to WhatsApp
- `bancard` → real Bancard API, falls back to WhatsApp
- `stripe` → USD-converted Stripe Checkout, falls back to WhatsApp
- `whatsapp` / `transfer` → instant confirmation + redirect

Always include sandbox fallback: if API keys aren't set, return `{ ok: true, sandbox: true, method: 'whatsapp', ... }` instead of an error. The site should work without any payment keys.

### Dynamic Products with JSON Fallback
When migrating from static JSON to DB products, use this pattern so admin edits appear live but the page still works if Supabase is down:

```tsx
const [dbProducts, setDbProducts] = useState([])
useEffect(() => {
  supabase.from("ej_products").select("*").then(({ data }) => {
    if (data?.length) setDbProducts(data.map(p => ({
      name: p.name, price: p.price, imageUrl: p.image_url, // camelCase mapping
    })))
  })
}, [])
const allProducts = dbProducts.length > 0 ? dbProducts : staticProducts
```

## Common Pitfalls from El Viajero Migration

### Supabase REST API auth issue: service_role key can't access table
This happens when the table doesn't exist OR RLS blocks the query even for service_role. Fix: check table exists FIRST, then check RLS.

### Client-Side Login → Middleware Session Mismatch

When a Next.js app uses `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` on a client component, the session cookie is set via the Supabase JS SDK's internal cookie handling. But the **Next.js middleware** (which calls `createMiddlewareClient()` from `@supabase/ssr`) needs the cookie to be readable via `req.cookies.getAll()`. If the client-side login doesn't trigger a full HTTP request (e.g., uses `router.push()` instead of `window.location.href`), the middleware never sees the cookie and redirects back to login.

**Symptom**: User logs in successfully (no error), sees redirect to /mi-cuenta, but the server-side middleware at /admin or /mi-cuenta checks the session and finds none → redirect back to /login. The user is stuck in a login loop.

**Root cause**: `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` sets cookies via `Set-Cookie` in a fetch response to the Supabase Auth API. The browser stores the cookie. But `router.push()` is a client-side transition — no new HTTP request is made. The middleware runs on the server side and reads `req.cookies` which is a snapshot of cookies at request time. If the user was redirected from `/login` (where they had no cookie) to `/mi-cuenta` via `router.push()`, the middleware sees the old request's cookies — which don't include the session.

**Fix**: After successful login, use `window.location.href = "/mi-cuenta"` instead of `router.push()`. This forces a full server-side navigation, sending the new cookies with the request. The middleware then reads them correctly.

```tsx
// BAD - client-side redirect, middleware doesn't get new cookie
if (res.ok) router.push("/mi-cuenta")

// GOOD - full server roundtrip, middleware reads cookie
if (res.ok) window.location.href = "/mi-cuenta"
```

**Also fix**: The admin layout's signOut button: `await supabase.auth.signOut(); window.location.href = "/login"` — because the order matters. `signOut()` clears the Supabase session locally, and the full navigation ensures cookies are cleared server-side.

**Verification**: After login, check `document.cookie` in browser console for the `sb-*-auth-token` cookie. Then check `fetch("/api/auth", { method: "POST", body: JSON.stringify({ action: "me" }) })` returns user data. If it returns "No hay sesión", the cookie isn't being sent/received properly.

### ej_site_config Table for Runtime Content

When building a content editor that needs to override static JSON content without a rebuild:

1. Create table: `CREATE TABLE ej_site_config (key text PRIMARY KEY, value jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}');`
2. Seed rows: content_overrides for editable fields, blog_posts for blog entries
3. The REST API won't see the table until schema cache refreshes (up to 10 min). To force: either click "Reload cache" in Supabase Dashboard > Settings > API, or run `ALTER TABLE ej_site_config ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` which triggers a cache refresh.
4. API routes: GET/POST for content_overrides, full CRUD for blog_posts
5. Frontend: import defaultContent from es.json at build time, merge with overrides from API at runtime
The builder's `products` table requires a valid `business_id`. Using `'00000000-...'` fails. Either create a `businesses` row, or (cleaner) use your own tables.

### 3. `npx supabase db push` Fails on Existing Migrations
When linking to a project that already has migrations from another app:
```bash
# First repair ALL existing migrations as "reverted"
npx supabase migration repair --status reverted <all-timestamps>
# Then push yours
npx supabase db push
```

### 4. DDL via Management API, not Service Key
The **service role key** does NOT work with `api.supabase.com`. Only the **personal access token** (`sbp_...`) works there.
Correct: `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer sbp_..." https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/{ref}/database/query`

### 5. read_file() Line-Number Corruption
`read_file()` returns content with `     1|import...` prefixed on each line. If you pass this to `write_file()`, those numbers become file content. Subsequent reads compound (`     1|     2|     3|...`). Use `patch()` for targeted edits. If corrupted: `sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*[0-9]\\+| //' file.tsx`

### 6. Auth-Aware Header Pattern
The navbar MUST detect auth state via Supabase session and swap:
- **Logged out**: Green "Ingresar" button with person icon → `/login`
- **Logged in**: User initials circle → hover dropdown with name, email, quick links (orders/addresses/favorites/settings), admin link if `role === 'admin'`, logout button
- **Mobile**: User card with pill buttons (Mi Cuenta, Pedidos, Admin, Salir) or full-width login CTA
- No localStorage auth checks — everything through Supabase `onAuthStateChange`

### 7. Unified Checkout Route
Single POST `/api/checkout` dispatches by `method` field (pagopar/bancard/stripe/whatsapp/transfer). Every gateway has a sandbox fallback: if no API keys set, returns `{ sandbox: true, method: 'whatsapp' }` instead of erroring. The site must work without payment keys configured.

useEffect(() => {

    if (data?.length) setDbProducts(data.map(toCamelCase))
  })

`node:20-alpine` does not come with wget. Either `RUN apk add --no-cache wget` or change the healthcheck to use node's built-in fetch.

### 11. User-Preference: Admin Theme Unification

**Symptom**: Mixing white theme (bg-white, text-gray-800) in some admin pages with dark theme (bg-gray-900, text-white) in others. The `AdminShell` and `AdminSidebar` components use white; new admin pages use dark. Result: jarring mismatch when navigating.

**Fix**: Set both `AdminShell` and `AdminSidebar` to dark theme:
- `admin-layout.tsx`: `bg-gray-950` (main), `bg-gray-900` (header), white text, gray borders
- `sidebar.tsx`: `bg-gray-900`, `text-gray-400`, active items `text-green-400 bg-green-600/20`
- All admin pages use consistent card classes: `rounded-xl border border-gray-800 bg-gray-900 p-4`

**Detection**: `grep -n 'bg-white\|bg-gray-50\|text-gray-800\|text-gray-600' app/admin/components/admin/*.tsx`

### 12. Stale Supabase API Keys
  
**Symptom**: Both anon and service_role keys return `"Invalid API key"` on every REST endpoint. This can happen mid-session — a key that worked moments ago suddenly stops working. The user may have regenerated keys in the Supabase Dashboard.

**Diagnosis**:
```bash
# Test anon key
curl -s "https://$SUPABASE_URL/rest/v1/profiles?select=count" \
  -H "apikey: $ANON_KEY" -H "Authorization: Bearer $ANON_KEY"
# If returns {"message":"Invalid API key"} → key is stale
```

**Fix**: User must go to Supabase Dashboard > Project Settings > API and copy the new keys. No way to recover old keys. Update both `.env.local` AND `content/es.json` (if anon key is duplicated there). Then rebuild + deploy:
```bash
export SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=<new_key>
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml <stack>
docker service update <stack>_web --force
```

### 12. Docker Service Update Doesn't Restart Containers (Same-Image-Tag Trap)

**Symptom**: After `docker build -t app:prod && docker stack deploy`, the running containers still serve the OLD code. Browser gets old CSS hashes (404), old icons, old content.

**Root cause**: Docker Swarm compares by image digest, not by content. If the image tag hasn't changed (`:prod` stays `:prod`), Swarm may skip restarting tasks if it detects no change in the service definition.

**Fix**:
```bash
# Force restart regardless of image digest comparison
docker service update <stack>_web --force
```

**Verification** that new image is running:
```bash
# Check running container has expected build artifacts
docker exec $(docker ps -q -f name=<stack>) ls .next/static/css/
# Compare with local
ls .next/static/css/
```

### 13. Per-Admin-Page Migration Misses Some Pages

When migrating admin pages from `createClient()` to `fetch("/api/admin/...")`, it's easy to miss pages that aren't in the main admin tree. Always search for ALL supabase `from("ej_` calls in the entire admin directory:
```bash
grep -rn 'from("ej_' app/admin/ --include='*.tsx' | grep -v node_modules
grep -rn 'createClient' app/admin/ --include='*.tsx' | grep -v node_modules
```

Pages known to be missed: categorias, usuarios, clientes, suscriptores. These typically use `ej_categories`, `profiles`, `ej_customers`, `ej_subscribers` tables via anon key.
### 14. Schema Cache Doesn't Refresh After CREATE TABLE

**Symptom**: Created a new table via Supabase Dashboard SQL Editor but REST API returns "Could not find the table in the schema cache" (HTTP 404).

**Fix chain**: Try `NOTIFY pgrst, 'reload schema';` first, then run any DDL on the new table (`ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;`), and finally use the "Reload Schema Cache" button in Settings > API. See `references/schema-cache-refresh.md`.

**Prevention**: Use a JSONB key-value table (ej_site_config) instead of creating new tables per feature. One DDL session, then every "new table" is just a new key.

### 15. No-DDL JSONB Content Store Pattern

When you can't run DDL (no supabase access token, or schema cache issues), store content overrides and blog posts in a single JSONB key-value table. See `references/content-editor-pattern.md` for the full Content Editor + Blog Manager implementation.

### 16. Content Overrides Pattern (No-DDL Admin Content Editing)

Frontend imports `content/es.json` statically at build time. Content editor stores overrides in DB table `ej_site_config` under the `content_overrides` key. On page load, merge: `value = overrides[path] || defaultContent[path]`. This means admin content changes are instant — no rebuild needed.

The `references/content-editor-pattern.md` file contains a complete implementation guide including the Content Editor page, Blog Manager, API routes, and theme unification pattern.

grep -rn 'createClient' app/admin/ --include='*.tsx' | grep -v node_modules
```

Known misses: categorias, usuarios, clientes, suscriptores. Create API routes for each missing resource, then patch the page to remove `createClient` import + replace supabase calls with fetch.

### 18. Service Worker Caches POST Requests

**Symptom**: Console errors: `TypeError: Failed to execute 'put' on 'Cache': Request method 'POST' is unsupported`

**Fix**: Add method check in sw.js fetch event handler:

```javascript
self.addEventListener('fetch', (event) => {
  if (event.request.method !== 'GET') {
    event.respondWith(fetch(event.request));
    return;
  }
  // ... rest of caching logic
});
```

## Session Issue Pattern

**Problem**: `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` in a `"use client"` component → `router.push()` → middleware doesn't see session → infinite redirect loop to `/login`.

**Why**: The Supabase JS SDK sets the session in the URL fragment (`#access_token=...`) after OAuth or password auth, but HTTP-only session cookies are only set when `updateSession()` runs in middleware during a **server navigation**. `router.push()` is client-side — no server trip → no cookies.

**Fix Chain**:
1. Change `router.push("/mi-cuenta")` to `window.location.href = "/mi-cuenta"` in login/register forms
2. Side effect: removes the smooth client-side transition, but makes auth actually work
3. Also works for logout: `await supabase.auth.signOut(); window.location.href = "/login"`
